title: Small Value Bet on a Dry Board
actionHistory: You raised to 25 from CO, BB called. Flop Kc-7s-2d.

options.optionExplanation: Small bet (1/3 pot) on a dry board extracts value from a wide range of worse hands.
Full pot is too large on this dry board. You fold out all the hands you want to keep in.
Checking top pair on a dry board gives up significant value.

explanation: On dry, disconnected boards like K-7-2 rainbow, small bet sizing is optimal. Why? Because there are no draws to charge, and your opponent's calling range is mostly weak pairs and ace-high hands. A small bet of 1/3 pot keeps all those marginal hands in — they'll call 17 with A7, pocket pairs, even ace-high. But if you bet 50 (full pot), those same hands fold, and you only get called by hands that beat you or are very strong. The principle: on dry boards, bet small because you want calls from weak hands, not folds. Your goal is to extract maximum value from the widest range of worse hands.
keyConceptSummary: On dry boards with no draws, bet small (1/3 pot) to keep weaker hands in the pot. Big bets only get called by strong hands.
villainRange: BB defend: Kx, 77, 22, A7, small pairs, ace-high

title: Large Bet on a Wet Draw-Heavy Board
actionHistory: You raised to 25 from BTN, BB called. Flop Jh-Th-8c.

options.optionExplanation: Large bet (80% pot) on a wet board denies equity to draws and charges them the maximum.
Too small. You're giving flush draws and straight draws a cheap price to outdraw you.
Checking aces on this dangerous board gives free cards to numerous draws that beat you.

explanation: On Jh-Th-8c, the board is loaded with draws: flush draws (any two hearts), open-ended straight draws (Q9, 97, KQ), gutshots (K9, A9, 76), and combo draws. Your AA is the best hand right now but extremely vulnerable. A large bet (75-100% pot) accomplishes two things: it charges draws the maximum price (making their calls unprofitable) and it builds a big pot while you're ahead. If you bet small, a hand like Qh9h gets an amazing price to call — and they have a flush draw plus an open-ended straight draw, giving them roughly 50% equity. Make them pay. The rule: wet boards with many possible draws demand larger bet sizes.
keyConceptSummary: On wet, draw-heavy boards, bet large (75-100% pot) to deny equity to draws. Small bets give opponents profitable prices to chase.
villainRange: BB on J-T-8: flush draws, straight draws, JT, J8, T8, sets, combo draws

title: River Value Bet — Choosing the Right Size
actionHistory: You raised preflop BTN, BB called. Flop Ac-9s-4d, you bet 25, BB called. Turn 2h, you bet 40, BB called. River 7c.

options.optionExplanation: A medium river bet (40% pot) targets the hands that can call — weaker aces and nines.
Full pot is too large. You only get called by AK and AQ, which beat you.
Checking misses value from the many worse hands that called two streets.

explanation: River bet sizing is about asking: what hands can call me, and how much will they call? Your AJ is strong but not the nuts. BB called the flop and turn, so their range includes: weaker aces (AT, A8, A5), nines (K9, Q9, 98), and maybe stubborn pocket pairs (TT, JJ). A 40% pot bet of 50 gets called by all those hands. A full pot bet of 120 scares off everything except AK and AQ (which beat you). The river sizing principle: bet what you think worse hands will call. Thin value bets should be smaller than thick value bets. With the nuts, you can bet big. With a good-but-not-great hand, bet smaller to target the widest range of callers.
keyConceptSummary: On the river, size your value bets based on what worse hands will call. Thin value = smaller bet. Thick value = larger bet.
villainRange: BB river range: A9-A2, K9, Q9, 98, TT, JJ

title: Preflop 3-Bet Sizing — Position Matters
actionHistory: CO raises to 25. Action on you on the BTN.

options.optionExplanation: 3x the open raise (75) is standard 3-bet sizing in position. Builds the pot while keeping opponent's range wide.
5x is too large in position. You narrow villain's range to only the strongest hands.
Just calling with KK lets the blinds in cheaply and fails to build the pot preflop.

explanation: 3-bet sizing depends on your position. In position (like the BTN), you should 3-bet to roughly 3× the open raise. Here that's 3 × 25 = 75. Why 3× and not bigger? Because you have position for the rest of the hand, which is a huge advantage. A smaller 3-bet keeps villain's calling range wider (they'll call with hands like AJ, KQ, TT that they'd fold to a 5× 3-bet), and you can outplay them postflop with your positional advantage. If you were out of position (like in the BB), you'd want to 3-bet to 4× (100) because you need a bigger pot to compensate for your positional disadvantage. Never just flat call with KK preflop — you need to build the pot with the best hand.
keyConceptSummary: 3-bet to ~3× the open raise in position, ~4× out of position. Position advantage allows smaller 3-bet sizes.
villainRange: CO open: TT+, ATs+, AJo+, KQs, KJs

title: Blocking Bet on a Scary River
actionHistory: BTN raised, you called BB. Flop Js-8d-3c, you check-called 25. Turn 5h, both checked. River Ah.

options.optionExplanation: A small blocking bet controls the pot. If BTN has an ace, they may just call instead of betting 70+.
Checking lets BTN bet any size they want. You'll face a tough decision against a large bet.
Betting big with QQ on an ace-high river is terrible. You only get called by hands that beat you.

explanation: The river ace is a nightmare card for your QQ. You were ahead on the turn but now any Ax in villain's range has you beaten. A blocking bet (small bet, 20-25% pot) is a clever tool in this spot. By betting 25 into 100, you achieve several things: (1) If villain has an ace, they might just call 25 instead of betting 70-80 themselves — saving you 50 chips. (2) If villain has a weaker hand like KJ or TT, they might call the small bet when they'd have just checked back. (3) You define the price of seeing a showdown rather than letting villain dictate the action. Blocking bets work best when you're out of position with a hand that might be good but can't handle a large bet.
keyConceptSummary: Blocking bets (20-25% pot) control the price of showdown when you're out of position with a vulnerable medium-strength hand.
villainRange: BTN range after line: Ax, KJ, QT, TT, 99, some missed draws

title: Overbet Shove — Polarized River Bet
actionHistory: You raised BTN, BB called. Flop 9c-6d-2h, BB check-called 35. Turn Kd, BB check-called 55. River 3s.

options.optionExplanation: All-in overbet with a set on a safe river. BB's calling range is weighted toward Kx which will struggle to fold.
Too small with a monster. You're leaving value on the table from kings that will call much more.
Better than 75 but still too small. With 300 behind in a 150 pot, maximizing value means shoving.

explanation: You have a set of nines — a monster on this board. BB check-called the flop and turn, and their range is heavily weighted toward kings (AK, KQ, KJ, KT) after the turn king. The river 3s is a blank. With 300 chips behind in a 150-chip pot, this is a perfect overbet spot. Why? Because your hand is at the top of your range (polarized strong), and BB's likely hand (a king) is strong enough to call a big bet but not strong enough to beat your set. An overbet jams maximum value from these hands. BB with AK is not folding for 300 into 150 — top pair top kicker feels too strong. That's exactly what you want. Overbets work when your opponents have hands too strong to fold but too weak to beat you.
keyConceptSummary: Overbet (more than pot) when you have a very strong hand and your opponent likely holds a hand too strong to fold but too weak to win.
villainRange: BB river range: AK, KQ, KJ, KT, 66, rare bluffs

title: Half-Pot Bluff on the River
actionHistory: You raised CO, BB called. Flop Kd-9c-6h, you bet 20, BB called. Turn 2h, you bet 30 with flush draw, BB called. River Js, missed flush.

options.optionExplanation: Half-pot bluff on a scare card river. The jack completes possible straights and you represent KJ or JJ.
Giving up is reasonable, but the jack is a great bluff card and you have no showdown value.
Full pot risks too much for a bluff. Smaller sizing achieves similar fold equity with less risk.

explanation: Your flush draw missed, leaving you with ace-high — no showdown value. But the river Js is a great bluffing card because it completes QT straights and hits hands like KJ, JJ, J9 that you could easily have in your range as the preflop raiser. When bluffing the river, sizing matters enormously. A half-pot bet (45 into 80) needs to work only 36% of the time to break even (45/125 = 36%). That's much better than a full-pot bluff that needs to work 50% of the time. The principle: use the minimum bet size that gets your opponent to fold. Larger bluffs risk more without proportionally increasing fold equity. Save your big bets for value; bluff efficiently with smaller sizes.
keyConceptSummary: When bluffing, use efficient sizing. Half-pot bluffs only need to work 36% of the time — less risk for similar fold equity.
villainRange: BB river range: Kx, 99, 66, 98, T9 — many will fold to a bluff on the J

title: Preflop Open Raise Sizing — Position Adjustment
actionHistory: Action is on you in UTG. No one has acted yet.

options.optionExplanation: Standard 2.5BB open from UTG. Clean, effective, and the modern standard sizing.
5BB open is way too large. You bloat the pot out of position and telegraph hand strength.
Limping JJ from UTG is a major mistake. You need to raise to build the pot and narrow the field.

explanation: Modern poker uses standardized open-raise sizing: 2-2.5× the big blind from all positions. Here, 2.5BB = 25 chips. Why not raise bigger with a premium hand like JJ? Because varying your raise size based on hand strength gives away information. If you raise to 50 with JJ but 25 with ATs, observant opponents will exploit you. By using the same sizing for your entire range, you're impossible to read preflop. The 2.5BB sizing also gives you a good risk-to-reward ratio: you risk 25 to win the 15 in blinds. From UTG specifically, some players use 2.5BB (instead of 2BB from the button) to discourage callers since you'll be out of position postflop.
keyConceptSummary: Use a standard open-raise size (2-2.5BB) regardless of hand strength. Consistent sizing prevents opponents from reading your hand.
villainRange: N/A — preflop open, no villain action yet

title: Pot-Sized Bet to Deny Equity
actionHistory: You raised CO, BB called. Flop Jh-Th-4c, you bet 35, BB called. Turn 5s.

options.optionExplanation: Pot-sized bet denies equity to the many draws. Flush draws and straight draws must pay maximum to continue.
Too small. You're giving draws a great price. A heart draw gets ~35% equity for only 29% pot odds.
Checking gives free cards to numerous draws. QQ is strong enough to bet for value and protection.

explanation: The board is Jh-Th-4c-5s and you have QQ — an overpair that's currently ahead but extremely vulnerable. BB's calling range from the flop includes flush draws (any two hearts), straight draws (KQ, K9, Q9, 89, 98), combo draws, and pairs with draws. A pot-sized bet of 100 into 100 forces draws to call 100 to win 200, needing 33% equity. Most draws have exactly or slightly less than 33% equity on the turn (flush draw = 19%, OESD = 17%, combo draw = 33%). By betting pot, you make it mathematically unprofitable for almost all draws to continue. If you bet only 40, they need just 29% — and many draws exceed that. Bet sizing is your weapon for denying equity.
keyConceptSummary: On wet boards with many draws, size up to pot or near-pot to deny profitable calls. Make opponents pay for their draws.
villainRange: BB turn range: flush draws, straight draws, JT, J4s, TT, sets, combo draws

title: Min-Raise Trap on the River
actionHistory: BTN raised, you called BB. Flop 8c-5d-2s, you check-called 20. Turn 4h (you made a straight), you checked, BTN checked. River Kc, you check, BTN bets 45.

options.optionExplanation: Min-raise to 100 traps BTN into calling. They already committed 45 and a small raise looks like a bluff.
Just calling with the nuts leaves value on the table. BTN bet, so they have something.
All-in for 350 into 135 is way too large. BTN folds everything except monsters.

explanation: You flopped an open-ended straight draw, turned the nut straight (8-7-6-5-4), and now the river Kc likely improved BTN's hand (they might have AK, KQ, KJ). BTN bets 45 — perfect. Now you need to extract maximum value. A min-raise to 100 (raise of 55 on top of their 45) is ideal here. Why? Because BTN just bet, signaling they have a hand. A small raise looks suspicious — almost like a bluff. BTN thinks 'they're trying to steal my pot with a raise, I have top pair.' They call. An all-in shove of 350 screams strength and folds everything except rare monsters. The min-raise is a trap that exploits opponents' tendency to call small raises while folding to large ones.
keyConceptSummary: Min-raises on the river with the nuts look like bluffs. Small raises extract more value than shoves because opponents are more likely to call.
villainRange: BTN river bet: AK, KQ, KJ, 88, overpairs, bluffs
